statement.en: Cyberbullying and Free Speech Considerations Under the New Jersey Anti-Bullying Bill of Rights Act
statement.summary: While cyberbullying shares many characteristics with traditional (offline) bullying (e.g., intention, aggressiveness, power imbalance, repetition), it can be particularly pernicious because of the medium in which it occurs. 3 As examples, a discrete incident of aggression that may have previously happened at a single time or place is not so constrained on the internet. 4 The abusive content itself can take the form of a comment, image, or recording. 5 And such aggressive content can be "repeatedly viewed, shared, saved, commented on, or 'Liked' by others, and result in a cycle of repetitionand associated ongoing damage or trauma for a victim who re-lives the experience." 6Additionally, the lack of non-verbal cues make intention more difficult to discern, and the power imbalance that is oft

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: The axiom presents a genuine dialectical tension: cyberbullying harm is empirically documentable (supporting regulatory intervention), yet free speech protections create irreducible legal/philosophical conflict. No resolution is theoretically dominant—jurisdiction, intent standards, and platform liability remain contested across legal systems.

ramanujanFormula.interpretation: デジタル空間では、小さな意図が無限に増幅され、決して消えない。

visualGlyph.interpretation: 中心の傷が波紋のように無限に広がり、どの方向にも逃げ場がない。
